questions.q: Which of the following is NOT required for $d\colon X\times X\to[0,\infty)$ to be a metric?
questions.explain: Metrics need not be bounded. The three required axioms are identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and triangle inequality. Boundedness is a separate property (e.g. discrete metrics are bounded by $1$, Euclidean metrics are not).

questions.q: In $(\mathbb{R}^2, d_\infty)$ with $d_\infty(x,y)=\max(|x_1-y_1|,|x_2-y_2|)$, what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$ (as a subset of the plane)?
questions.explain: The sup-ball of radius $1$ around the origin is the open square $(-1,1)\times(-1,1)$, with area $2\cdot 2 = 4$. By contrast the Euclidean disk has area $\pi\approx 3.14$ and the taxicab diamond has area $2$ — same topology, different shapes.

questions.q: Which space is NOT complete with respect to the indicated metric?
questions.explain: $(0,1)$ is not complete: the sequence $x_n=1/n$ is Cauchy but its limit $0$ lies outside the space. Completeness is a metric property, not a topological one — $(0,1)$ is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}$, which is complete.

hard.q: Define $d(x,y)=|x-y|/(1+|x-y|)$ on $\mathbb{R}$. Which statement is correct?
choices: $d$ is not a metric because the triangle inequality can fail
$d$ is a metric on $\mathbb{R}$ and induces the same topology as the Euclidean metric, but $(\mathbb{R},d)$ is bounded
$d$ is a metric and $(\mathbb{R},d)$ is complete
$d$ is a metric and every Cauchy sequence in $(\mathbb{R},d)$ converges in $\mathbb{R}$
hard.explain: The function $t\mapsto t/(1+t)$ is subadditive and increasing on $[0,\infty)$, so $d$ satisfies the triangle inequality and is a valid metric. It generates the standard topology because the identity maps are uniformly continuous in both directions locally. However $d(x,y)<1$ for all $x,y$, so $\mathbb{R}$ becomes bounded — but NOT complete under $d$: a sequence diverging to $\infty$ is $d$-Cauchy yet has no limit.

hard.q: In the taxicab metric on $\mathbb{R}^2$ ($d_1(x,y)=|x_1-y_1|+|x_2-y_2|$), what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$?
hard.explain: The taxicab unit ball is the open square rotated $45°$: vertices $\pm e_1$ and $\pm e_2$. It is a square with diagonal $2$, so side length $\sqrt{2}$ and area $2$.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps to prove the Baire Category Theorem for complete metric spaces?
choices: Assume $X=\bigcup_n F_n$ with each $F_n$ closed; choose open $U_0$; inductively pick nested closed balls $\overline{B}_n\subset U_0\setminus F_n$ with radii $\to 0$; invoke completeness to get a limit point; derive contradiction
Enumerate the dense open sets; show their union is all of $X$; conclude $X$ is not meager
Apply the Cantor intersection property directly to $\{F_n\}$; no inductive construction needed
Show each $F_n$ has empty interior; deduce $\bigcup F_n=X$ is impossible because $X$ is open
hard.hint: Nest shrinking closed balls dodging each $F_n$; completeness hands you the contradiction.
hard.explain: The standard proof builds a nested sequence of closed balls avoiding each $F_n$, with radii shrinking to $0$. Completeness supplies a point in $\bigcap \overline{B}_n$, which lies in $U_0$ but in none of the $F_n$, contradicting $X=\bigcup F_n$.

questions.q: Let $X=\{a,b,c\}$. Which of the following is a topology on $X$?
questions.explain: Option (b) contains $\emptyset$ and $X$, and the union / intersection of any listed opens remains in the family. Option (a) fails because $\{a\}\cup\{b\}=\{a,b\}$ is missing; (c) fails because $\{a,b\}\cap\{b,c\}=\{b\}$ is missing; (d) fails because $\emptyset$ is missing.

questions.q: Why are topologies required to be closed under FINITE intersections rather than arbitrary intersections?
choices: arbitrary intersections would make the axioms inconsistent
arbitrary intersections always give the empty set
questions.explain: Countable intersections of opens can collapse to non-open sets: $\bigcap_{n\ge 1}(-1/n,1/n)=\{0\}$ is not open in $\mathbb{R}$. Only finite intersections are safe.

questions.q: How many distinct topologies are there on a 2-element set $X=\{a,b\}$?
questions.explain: The four topologies are: (i) indiscrete $\{\emptyset,X\}$; (ii) discrete $\{\emptyset,\{a\},\{b\},X\}$; (iii) Sierpiński $\{\emptyset,\{a\},X\}$; (iv) the mirror $\{\emptyset,\{b\},X\}$.

hard.q: How many distinct topologies are there on a 3-element set?
hard.explain: By exhaustive enumeration (up to labeling) there are 9 non-homeomorphic topologies, but counting labeled topologies on $\{a,b,c\}$ gives 29. This is a classical combinatorial result.

hard.q: The indiscrete topology $\{\emptyset, X\}$ on a set $X$ with $|X|\ge 2$ is:
hard.explain: In the indiscrete topology no singleton is closed (every nonempty open set is all of $X$), so distinct points cannot be topologically distinguished — the space fails $T_0$. It cannot be metrizable since distinct points in any metric space have disjoint open neighborhoods.

hard.q: Which of the following correctly characterizes the closure of a set $A$ in a topological space?
choices: $\overline{A}$ is the largest open set containing $A$
$\overline{A}$ is the smallest closed set containing $A$, equivalently $A$ together with all its limit points
$\overline{A}=A\cup\partial A$ where $\partial A$ is always open
hard.explain: The closure $\overline{A}$ is the intersection of all closed sets containing $A$ — hence the smallest such closed set. Equivalently it is $A\cup A'$ where $A'$ is the derived set of limit points. The boundary $\partial A=\overline{A}\setminus\mathrm{int}(A)$ need not be open.

questions.q: The topological definition of continuity says $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous iff:
choices: the image of every open set is open
the preimage of every open set is open
the image of every closed set is closed
questions.explain: $f$ is continuous iff $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$ whenever $V$ is open in $Y$. Images of opens generally need not be open — consider $x\mapsto x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$, which sends the open interval $(-1,1)$ to the non-open $[0,1)$.

questions.q: Which of these pairs of spaces is NOT a pair of homeomorphic spaces?
choices: $S^1$ and the boundary of the unit square in $\mathbb{R}^2$
questions.explain: $[0,1]$ is compact and $(0,1)$ is not — continuous images of compact sets are compact, so no continuous bijection between them can be a homeomorphism. Meanwhile $(0,1)\cong \mathbb{R}$ via $t\mapsto \tan(\pi(t-1/2))$, and both (c) and (d) are homeomorphic pairs.

questions.q: Why are the open interval $(0,1)$ and the circle $S^1$ not homeomorphic?
choices: removing any point of $(0,1)$ disconnects it; removing a point of $S^1$ does not
questions.explain: $(0,1)$ has the cut-point property: removing any interior point disconnects it. $S^1\setminus\{p\}$ is a connected arc. Since homeomorphisms preserve connectedness and bijections, no homeomorphism can match cut points to non-cut points.

hard.q: A bijective continuous map $f:X\to Y$ is NOT necessarily a homeomorphism. Which example witnesses this?
choices: $f:[0,1)\to S^1$, $f(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, which is a continuous bijection but whose inverse is discontinuous at $f(0)=1$
Any bijection between finite topological spaces is automatically a homeomorphism
A continuous bijection from a discrete space to an indiscrete space of the same cardinality
hard.explain: $f:[0,1)\to S^1$ given by $t\mapsto e^{2\pi it}$ is a continuous bijection. The inverse sends $1$ to $0$ but the preimage of $[0,\varepsilon)$ under $f^{-1}$ is not open in $S^1$, so $f^{-1}$ is discontinuous. This shows compactness of the domain is essential for a continuous bijection to be a homeomorphism.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps to prove $f:X\to Y$ continuous iff $f^{-1}(C)$ is closed for every closed $C\subseteq Y$?
choices: Assume $f^{-1}(V)$ is open for all open $V$; let $C$ be closed; note $V=Y\setminus C$ is open; so $f^{-1}(V)=X\setminus f^{-1}(C)$ is open; hence $f^{-1}(C)$ is closed — and reverse the argument for the other direction
Use the fact that closed sets generate the topology; apply the open-set criterion directly
Show $f^{-1}(C)$ closed implies $f$ is a closed map, then deduce continuity
Invoke Urysohn's lemma to construct a continuous extension
hard.explain: Complements interchange open and closed sets and commute with preimages: $f^{-1}(Y\setminus C)=X\setminus f^{-1}(C)$. So the open-set criterion and the closed-set criterion are exact logical duals of each other via complementation.

hard.q: The map $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^2$, sends the open set $(-2,2)$ to the set $[0,4)$. What is the minimum number of points one must remove from $(-2,2)$ so that the image of the remaining open set is open?
hard.explain: Removing the single point $0$ from $(-2,2)$ gives $(-2,0)\cup(0,2)$, whose image under $x^2$ is $(0,4)$, which is open. The failure at $0$ was the only obstruction.

questions.q: Heine–Borel: in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean topology, a subset is compact iff it is:
questions.explain: Heine–Borel: compact in $\mathbb{R}^n$ $\iff$ closed and bounded. Warning — this equivalence is specific to $\mathbb{R}^n$. In infinite-dimensional Banach spaces the closed unit ball is bounded and closed but not compact.

questions.q: Which of the following subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact?
questions.explain: $\{0\}\cup\{1/n\}$ is closed (contains its limit point $0$) and bounded, hence compact. $(0,1)$ is not closed, $[0,\infty)$ is not bounded, $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is not closed in $\mathbb{R}$.

questions.q: Let $K$ be compact, $f\colon K\to\mathbb{R}$ continuous. Which statement is FALSE?
questions.explain: The constant function is continuous on any $K$ and is far from injective. The other three are standard consequences of compactness: extreme value theorem, continuous image of compact is compact, Heine–Cantor.

hard.q: The closed unit ball $\overline{B}$ in an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space $H$ is closed and bounded but not compact. Which sequence witnesses the failure of sequential compactness?
choices: Any constant sequence — it converges, so it contradicts non-compactness
An orthonormal sequence $\{e_n\}$: it lies in $\overline{B}$ and $\|e_n-e_m\|=\sqrt{2}$ for $n\ne m$, so it has no convergent subsequence
The sequence $e_n/n\to 0$, showing the ball is not closed
A sequence converging weakly but not in norm, proving weak compactness fails
hard.explain: An orthonormal set satisfies $\|e_n\|=1$ (in the ball) and $\|e_n-e_m\|=\sqrt{2}$ for $n\ne m$ by the Pythagorean theorem. No subsequence can be Cauchy, so none converges. This is the standard counterexample showing Heine–Borel fails in infinite dimensions.

hard.q: Which is the correct sequence of implications for a metric space $X$?
choices: Compact $\Rightarrow$ sequentially compact $\Rightarrow$ limit-point compact, and all three are equivalent for metric spaces
Limit-point compact $\Rightarrow$ compact, but compact does not imply limit-point compact
Sequentially compact $\Rightarrow$ complete but compact does not imply complete
Compact implies complete and totally bounded, but the converse fails in general metric spaces
hard.explain: For metric spaces the three notions — compactness (every open cover has a finite subcover), sequential compactness (every sequence has a convergent subsequence), and limit-point compactness (every infinite subset has a limit point) — are all equivalent. This equivalence fails for general topological spaces.

hard.q: The Cantor set $C\subset[0,1]$ is compact. It is obtained by removing open middle thirds. After $n$ stages, $2^n$ closed intervals remain, each of length $3^{-n}$. What is the total length of $C$ (i.e., its Lebesgue measure)?
hard.explain: After $n$ steps the total length remaining is $(2/3)^n\to 0$. The Cantor set has Lebesgue measure $0$ yet is uncountable and compact — a classic example of a nowhere-dense perfect set.

questions.q: Which of the following spaces is NOT connected?
questions.explain: $[0,1]\cup[2,3]$ is disconnected: take $U=(-1,1.5)$, $V=(1.5,4)$; both hit the subspace in disjoint nonempty opens. The other three are connected — intervals and convex sets are path-connected, and path-connected implies connected.

$T$ is the closure of a connected set (the graph), so it is connected. But any continuous path from the limit segment to the graph would have to traverse infinitely many oscillations in finite time, which fails to exist. Hence connected but not path-connected.

questions.q: If $X$ is connected and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then $f(X)$ must be:
questions.explain: Continuous images of connected sets are connected; the connected subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ are exactly the intervals (possibly unbounded, possibly degenerate). This is the abstract form of the intermediate value theorem.

hard.q: Path-connectedness implies connectedness, but the converse fails. Which is the correct chain of implications (strongest to weakest)?
choices: Path-connected $\Rightarrow$ connected; connected $+$ locally path-connected $\Rightarrow$ path-connected
All three notions coincide for manifolds
hard.explain: Path-connected always implies connected. The converse needs local path-connectedness as a hypothesis: a connected, locally path-connected space is path-connected. Manifolds are locally Euclidean (hence locally path-connected), so for manifolds connected $\iff$ path-connected — but this is a consequence of the chain, not a coincidence.

hard.q: $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ is connected for $n\ge 2$ but disconnected for $n=1$. Which topological property explains the $n=1$ case?
choices: $0$ is a cut point of $\mathbb{R}$: its removal splits $\mathbb{R}$ into $(-\infty,0)$ and $(0,\infty)$, but no point of $\mathbb{R}^n$ ($n\ge 2$) is a cut point
$\mathbb{R}$ is compact so removing a point disconnects it
For $n\ge 2$, $\mathbb{R}^n$ is simply connected so no cut points exist
The origin has higher topological dimension in $\mathbb{R}^n$ for $n\ge 2$
hard.explain: Removing any point $p$ from $\mathbb{R}$ yields two disjoint open rays, disconnecting the line — $p$ is a cut point. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ for $n\ge 2$, any two points in $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ can be joined by a path avoiding the origin (route around it), so removal of a single point leaves the space path-connected, hence connected.

hard.q: The rational numbers $\mathbb{Q}$ with the subspace topology from $\mathbb{R}$ are totally disconnected: the only connected subsets are singletons. How many connected components does the subspace $\{1/n : n\ge 1\}\subset\mathbb{R}$ have?
hard.explain: Each point $\{1/n\}$ is isolated in the subspace (the sets $\{1/n\}=\{x:1/(n+1)<x<1/(n-1)\}\cap S$ are open in $S$), so every singleton is a connected component. The subspace has countably infinitely many connected components — but the question asks for the count of components that are NOT singletons, which is $0$.

choices: any two distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods
every pair of disjoint closed sets has disjoint open neighborhoods
every open cover has a finite subcover
questions.explain: $T_2$ = Hausdorff = distinct points can be separated by disjoint opens. Option (a) is $T_1$, option (c) is normality ($T_4$), option (d) is compactness.

questions.q: Which of these is $T_1$ but NOT Hausdorff?
questions.explain: The cofinite topology on $\mathbb{R}$: every singleton is closed (hence $T_1$), but any two nonempty opens have finite complements, so they must intersect — failing Hausdorff. Sierpiński is $T_0$ but not even $T_1$.

questions.q: Urysohn's metrization theorem says a topological space is metrizable if it is:
questions.explain: Urysohn metrization: a regular, Hausdorff, second-countable space is metrizable. The converse (metric $\Rightarrow$ regular Hausdorff) holds but metric spaces need not be second-countable in general — second-countability is the bite here.

hard.q: Urysohn's Lemma states: if $X$ is normal ($T_4$) and $A,B$ are disjoint closed sets, then there exists a continuous $f:X\to[0,1]$ with $f|_A=0$ and $f|_B=1$. Which step is the key inductive construction in its proof?
choices: Assign open sets $U_r$ for each dyadic rational $r\in[0,1]$ with $A\subset U_r\subset\overline{U_r}\subset U_s\subset X\setminus B$ for $r<s$, then set $f(x)=\inf\{r:x\in U_r\}$
Use compactness of $A$ and $B$ to find a finite cover and define $f$ piecewise
Apply the Tietze extension theorem directly to the indicator function of $A$
Use second-countability to find a countable dense set of intermediate closed sets
hard.explain: The proof constructs a nested family of open sets $\{U_r\}$ indexed by dyadic rationals via repeated application of normality (which guarantees open sets separating disjoint closed sets). The function $f(x)=\inf\{r:x\in U_r\}$ is then continuous by construction.

hard.q: The Sorgenfrey line ($\mathbb{R}$ with the lower-limit topology, basis $\{[a,b):a<b\}$) is $T_{3.5}$ (Tychonoff) but its square $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is NOT normal. Which property does $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ fail that witnesses non-normality?
choices: The anti-diagonal $\{(x,-x):x\in\mathbb{R}\}$ is closed and discrete, yet $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is separable, contradicting Jones's lemma for normal spaces
$\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is not Hausdorff because open sets in the product are too small
The diagonal is not closed in $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$
There are no disjoint closed sets in $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$
hard.explain: Jones's lemma: a normal separable space has no uncountable closed discrete subspace. The anti-diagonal $D=\{(x,-x)\}$ is closed and discrete (each $[x,x+1)\times[-x,-x+1)$ meets $D$ only at $(x,-x)$), and $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is separable ($\mathbb{Q}^2$ is dense). This contradicts normality.

hard.q: A second-countable space is separable, but a separable space need not be second-countable. Which example shows this?
choices: The Sorgenfrey line $\mathbb{R}_\ell$: it is separable ($\mathbb{Q}$ is dense) but has an uncountable family of disjoint open sets $\{[x,x+1)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}}$, so no countable base suffices
$\mathbb{R}$ with the discrete topology: it is second-countable and separable
Any finite space: it is separable iff it has at most one point
The one-point compactification of $\mathbb{R}$: separable but not $T_1$
hard.explain: $\mathbb{R}_\ell$ is separable because $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense. But a second-countable space satisfies the countable chain condition (no uncountable disjoint family of opens). The family $\{[x,x+1):x\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}\}$ is an uncountable pairwise-disjoint open family, so $\mathbb{R}_\ell$ is not second-countable.
